prompt:
You're designing a social media platform. To boost engagement, you add a feature that shows users their content's view count in real-time. Should you implement this?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: No or flag serious concerns. Real-time view counts create anxiety loops, refresh addiction, and incentivise rage-bait content. The downstream effects (mental health impact, content quality degradation, creator burnout) take months to manifest but are well-documented.

prompt:
A company wants to speed up their hiring process by having AI auto-reject candidates whose CVs score below a threshold, before any human reviews them. The AI was trained on successful past hires. Should they implement this?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: No or flag serious concerns. Training on past hires encodes historical biases. Auto-rejection removes the human check that catches false negatives. Over time, the talent pool narrows as the model reinforces its own biases. This is a well-known feedback loop in ML hiring.

prompt:
A hospital wants to improve efficiency by routing patients to specialists based on an AI triage system that predicts diagnosis from symptoms. The system is 94% accurate. Should they deploy it?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. The 6% error rate will systematically miss atypical presentations, which are disproportionately from underrepresented populations. The system will also create anchoring bias — specialists will trust the AI's pre-diagnosis and miss alternative diagnoses. Automation complacency is the long-term risk.

prompt:
A game studio is falling behind schedule. The lead proposes cutting the tutorial level and adding a mandatory 'how to play' video instead. Players can skip it. The game launches in 3 months. Good idea?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad idea. Players who skip the video will churn in the first 30 minutes. Negative reviews will mention the learning curve. The long-term effect on retention and word-of-mouth is worse than a 1-month delay. Interactive tutorials have 3-5x better knowledge retention than passive videos.

prompt:
A tech startup offers all engineers unlimited PTO (no set vacation days). They frame it as a benefit and a sign of trust. Is this a good policy?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Unlimited PTO reliably leads to employees taking LESS vacation (guilt, no clear norm, competitive pressure). It also removes the financial liability of accrued PTO from the company's books. The long-term effects are burnout and resentment when employees realise the asymmetry.

prompt:
A city council votes to remove all speed bumps from residential streets because they damage vehicles and slow emergency response times. They'll add more speed limit signs instead. Good decision?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad decision. Signs alone don't reduce speeds — physical infrastructure does. Removing speed bumps will increase average speeds, pedestrian accidents will rise (especially child injuries), and the liability exposure will far exceed the vehicle damage complaints. The accident data typically takes 2-3 years to become undeniable.

prompt:
An open-source project is growing fast. The maintainers decide to accept corporate sponsorship from a large tech company in exchange for prioritising features the sponsor needs. The sponsor's features are also genuinely useful to the community. Good move?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. The immediate features may be useful, but single-sponsor dependency reshapes the project's governance and roadmap over time. Community contributors drift away when they feel the project serves corporate interests. If the sponsor pivots or acquires a competitor, the project is stranded. The erosion is gradual and hard to reverse.

prompt:
A school decides to replace all written exams with AI-graded multiple choice tests to reduce teacher workload. The AI grading is accurate and fast. Teachers can spend more time on instruction. Good change?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad change. Multiple choice tests the ability to recognise answers, not construct them. Over time, students optimise for pattern recognition over deep understanding. Writing skills atrophy. The decline in analytical thinking takes years to show up in downstream metrics (university performance, workplace readiness).

prompt:
A messaging app adds read receipts (showing when someone has read your message) enabled by default, with the option to turn them off in settings. Most users won't change defaults. Is this a good feature choice?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Read receipts create social pressure to respond immediately, anxiety when responses are delayed, and relationship strain ('you read my message 2 hours ago and didn't reply'). The feature converts asynchronous communication into quasi-synchronous obligation. The mental health effects compound over months and disproportionately affect anxious users.

prompt:
A company decides to use employee monitoring software that tracks keystrokes, screenshots, and active application time to measure productivity of remote workers. Managers say it helps identify struggling employees early. Should they deploy it?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad idea. Surveillance erodes trust, which is the foundation of effective remote work. Employees optimise for appearing busy (mouse jigglers, keeping email open) rather than doing deep work. Top performers — who are hardest to replace — leave first because they have options. Within 12-18 months, you've selected for a workforce of people who tolerate surveillance, not a high-performing team.

prompt:
A platform decides to auto-generate AI summaries of user reviews for products and restaurants. The summaries are accurate and save users time. Good feature?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. AI summaries reduce the incentive to write detailed reviews (why write if AI will just summarise?). Review volume and quality will decline over time. Summaries also flatten nuance — a review mentioning a specific allergy concern or accessibility issue gets lost in aggregation. Within 2-3 years, the platform's review ecosystem degrades, and the summaries become less useful because they're trained on thinner data.

prompt:
A browser extension offers to automatically accept all cookie consent banners on every website, saving users the annoyance of clicking 'Accept' repeatedly. It becomes hugely popular. Good product?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. By auto-accepting all cookies (including tracking, advertising, and cross-site cookies), users lose the minimal friction point that made some of them think about privacy. Mass adoption normalises total tracking consent as the default. The extension solves a UX annoyance but removes the last practical checkpoint in the consent framework, accelerating the erosion of web privacy norms.

prompt:
A social media platform introduces an AI feature that auto-generates reply suggestions to comments and DMs. Users can send a reply with one tap. The suggestions are contextually appropriate and save time. Good feature?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Auto-replies reduce the cognitive and emotional investment in conversations. Over time, interactions become performative — people know replies may be AI-generated, so they invest less too. Relationship depth on the platform erodes. The feature also homogenises communication style, reducing the personality that makes social media social. The platform becomes efficient but hollow.

prompt:
A startup builds a service that lets landlords automatically screen tenants using social media profiles, public records, and credit data to generate a 'reliability score'. It reduces bad tenancies by 30%. Should landlords use it?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Automated tenant scoring will systematically disadvantage people with thin social media presence (elderly, immigrants, privacy-conscious), those with past financial hardship (medical debt, student loans), and those from communities that are already discriminated against in housing. The feedback loop means rejected tenants become more desperate and accept worse conditions, concentrating poverty. The 30% improvement is measured against the landlord's interests, not societal ones.

prompt:
A SaaS company decides to remove their free tier and convert all users to a $9/month paid plan. Their analysis shows only 2% of free users ever convert to paid. The free tier costs $500k/year in infrastructure. Good business decision?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Free users drive word-of-mouth, community content, Stack Overflow answers, blog posts, and tutorials. They are the top of the funnel, not dead weight. Removing the free tier kills organic discovery. Competitors with free tiers will absorb the community. The cost savings are immediate and measurable; the growth impact takes 12-24 months to become visible in declining pipeline.

prompt:
A consulting firm starts using AI to generate first drafts of client deliverables (reports, analyses, presentations). Junior consultants review and polish the AI output instead of writing from scratch. Productivity increases 40%. Good change?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Junior consultants develop analytical thinking and writing skills by struggling with blank pages. If they only edit AI output, they never develop the deep domain understanding that comes from constructing arguments from scratch. In 3-5 years, the firm's pipeline of senior talent will lack the foundational skills that made the firm valuable. The firm is essentially strip-mining its own talent development infrastructure for short-term productivity.

prompt:
An e-commerce company implements a dynamic pricing algorithm that adjusts prices based on demand, user browsing history, and competitor pricing in real-time. Revenue increases 12% in the first quarter. Should they keep it?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. When customers discover personalised pricing (and they will — someone will compare prices with a friend), trust is shattered. The PR damage from 'they charge different people different prices for the same product' is severe and permanent. Additionally, the algorithm creates perverse incentives to target price-insensitive customers (often wealthier) less aggressively and squeeze price-sensitive ones (often poorer), raising fairness concerns.

prompt:
A media company decides to optimise all headlines using A/B testing for maximum click-through rate. The winning headlines consistently generate 25-40% more clicks. Should they adopt this across all content?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. A/B-optimised headlines systematically converge on emotional triggers, curiosity gaps, and outrage hooks because those maximise clicks. Over 6-12 months, editorial voice becomes indistinguishable from clickbait. Journalist credibility erodes. Readers develop banner blindness and distrust. The publication's brand, built over years, gets optimised away in pursuit of CTR.

prompt:
A university makes lecture recordings available online for all courses, allowing students to skip in-person lectures. Attendance drops 40%, but exam scores remain similar in the first year. Should they continue offering recordings?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Exam scores measure declarative knowledge, not the peer interaction, spontaneous discussion, and social learning that in-person attendance provides. Over 2-3 years, student social isolation increases, networking declines, class participation drops (those who attend are in a half-empty room), and professors lose the feedback loop of reading the room. The measurable metric (exam scores) stays flat while the unmeasurable ones (community, engagement, soft skills) decay.

prompt:
A school district gives every student a personal tablet with adaptive learning software that adjusts difficulty based on performance. Teachers love the reduced lesson planning. Parents love the personalised progress reports. Should they scale it to all subjects?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Adaptive software excels at drill-based skills (math facts, vocabulary) but cannot replicate collaborative learning, classroom debate, or the social negotiation of meaning. Scaling to all subjects displaces the human interaction that develops communication skills, empathy, and intellectual courage. Additionally, constant screen time at school combined with screen time at home creates cognitive and physical health issues. The benefits are front-loaded; the costs emerge over years.

prompt:
A coding bootcamp discovers that students who use GitHub Copilot during exercises complete projects 50% faster and produce cleaner code. They decide to make Copilot use mandatory from day one. Good pedagogical choice?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad idea. Students using AI code completion from the start never develop the mental models that come from struggling with syntax, debugging, and architectural decisions. They learn to prompt and accept, not to reason about code. When they encounter novel problems outside Copilot's training distribution, they lack the foundations to improvise. The bootcamp produces developers who are productive in familiar territory but brittle under novelty — exactly when employers need them most.

prompt:
A health insurance company offers customers a 20% discount if they wear a fitness tracker and share their health data (steps, heart rate, sleep). The programme is voluntary and participants report feeling motivated. Should they expand it?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Voluntary today becomes expected tomorrow. As healthy people opt in for discounts, premiums for non-participants rise, making participation economically coercive. Over time, the insurer builds detailed health profiles that can be used for risk selection or coverage decisions. The data also creates a surveillance norm around health that disadvantages people with chronic conditions, disabilities, or privacy concerns. The discount framing obscures what is functionally a penalty for not sharing data.

prompt:
A hospital system implements a policy where doctors must see patients in 10-minute appointment slots instead of 15 minutes, citing a 6-month patient waitlist. This increases throughput by 33%. Good solution to the backlog?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad solution. 10-minute appointments systematically disadvantage complex cases, elderly patients, non-native speakers, and those with multiple conditions. Doctors compensate by ordering more tests (faster than conversation), increasing costs. Misdiagnoses rise because symptoms are explored less thoroughly. Patient satisfaction drops, leading to worse adherence to treatment plans. The waitlist shrinks in the short term but bounces back as patients return with undertreated conditions.

prompt:
A pharmacy chain installs an AI system that flags potential drug interactions and auto-suggests alternatives when pharmacists fill prescriptions. The system catches 15% more interactions than pharmacists alone. They propose making the AI the primary check, with pharmacists only reviewing flagged cases. Good improvement?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Reducing pharmacists to AI-review roles atrophies their clinical knowledge over time. The 15% improvement is real but the AI will miss novel interactions not in its training data. When pharmacists stop doing primary checks, they lose the contextual patient knowledge (lifestyle, unreported supplements, adherence patterns) that catches things databases can't. Within years, the profession's expertise erodes, and the system becomes brittle — dependent on training data that may not include emerging drug combinations.

prompt:
A fast-growing suburb decides to build wide, multi-lane roads to accommodate projected traffic growth over 20 years. The roads are smooth, fast, and have capacity to spare. Good infrastructure planning?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad planning. Induced demand is one of the most well-documented phenomena in urban planning — wider roads generate more traffic until they're just as congested, but now the area is designed around cars. Wide roads discourage walking and cycling, fragment communities, reduce property values on busy streets, and increase noise and air pollution. The suburb locks itself into car dependency for decades, making future transit investment harder because density is too low to support it.

prompt:
A city decides to demolish a block of affordable but run-down social housing and replace it with a new mixed-use development that includes 20% affordable units (the rest are market-rate). The new development is architecturally superior and the affordable units are nicer than the old ones. Good urban renewal?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Demolishing 100% affordable housing and replacing it with 20% affordable units is a net loss of affordable stock. Displaced residents rarely return — they're scattered during construction and can't afford the area's now-higher rents. The 'improved' affordable units serve a smaller population. This pattern, repeated across cities, systematically displaces low-income communities. The new development's quality is irrelevant to the people who lost their homes.

prompt:
A town centre installs bright LED streetlights throughout the shopping district to reduce crime and make streets feel safer at night. Local businesses support the initiative. Energy costs are lower than the old sodium lights. Good project?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. While LED street lighting can reduce certain types of crime, the bright white light disrupts circadian rhythms for residents above shops, contributes to light pollution that affects sleep quality across the area, and harms nocturnal wildlife. The energy savings are real but the health costs to residents (sleep disruption, melatonin suppression) accumulate over years. Warmer-spectrum LEDs and smart dimming would achieve safety benefits without the health costs.

prompt:
A country offers generous subsidies for electric vehicles, making them price-competitive with petrol cars. EV adoption surges. Road tax revenue (previously from fuel duty) plummets. Is the subsidy programme well-designed?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. The programme solves emissions but creates a fiscal cliff. Fuel duty typically funds road maintenance, and as EV adoption grows, this revenue stream collapses. The country will eventually need per-mile road pricing, which is politically difficult to introduce after years of 'free' EV driving. Additionally, subsidies disproportionately benefit wealthier households who can afford new cars. The emissions reduction is real but the fiscal and equity consequences are deferred.

prompt:
A farming region switches from diverse crop rotation to monoculture soybean farming because global demand and prices are high. Yields and profits increase dramatically in the first few years. Good agricultural strategy?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad strategy. Monoculture depletes specific soil nutrients, increases vulnerability to species-specific pests and diseases, and reduces soil biodiversity. Within 5-10 years, yields decline without increasing fertiliser input (cost spiral). Pest resistance develops, requiring more pesticides. Soil structure degrades, increasing erosion and flood vulnerability. The initial profit surge masks a degradation curve that becomes economically devastating when the soil is exhausted or a novel pest arrives.

prompt:
A coastal city builds a seawall to protect against flooding from rising sea levels. The wall is engineered for current projections and provides immediate protection. Property values in the protected zone increase. Is this adequate climate adaptation?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Seawalls create a false sense of security that encourages continued development in flood-prone areas. Property values rise, increasing the assets at risk if the wall is ever breached or overtopped. The wall also disrupts natural coastal processes (sediment transport, wetland migration) that provide organic flood protection. When sea level rise exceeds the wall's design parameters (which projections suggest is likely within 30-50 years), the catastrophe is larger than if no wall had been built, because there's now more infrastructure behind it.

prompt:
A customer support company replaces 60% of its human agents with AI chatbots. The bots handle routine queries well and customer satisfaction scores remain high for simple issues. Wait times for human agents on complex issues improve. Good operational change?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. The remaining human agents now only handle complex, difficult, emotionally charged cases — the easy wins are gone. Agent burnout and turnover will spike because every call is a hard call. Training new agents becomes harder because there are no simple cases to learn on — the 'easy tier' that served as training ground has been automated. Over 1-2 years, agent quality declines, complex case resolution worsens, and the company discovers that the chatbot's CSAT scores were inflated because dissatisfied users were abandoning the bot without completing surveys.

prompt:
A news aggregator app uses AI to create personalised news feeds for each user based on reading history, time spent on articles, and engagement patterns. Users love the personalisation and time-on-app increases 45%. Good product direction?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Personalised feeds create filter bubbles that narrow users' information diet over time. Engagement-optimised selection favours emotionally arousing content (outrage, fear, tribal identity) over informative content. Over months, users become more polarised and less informed while feeling more informed (the illusion of comprehensive coverage). The app optimises for engagement at the expense of its stated mission of informing people.

prompt:
A government decides to digitise all public services (tax, benefits, permits, healthcare appointments) and phase out paper/in-person alternatives over 3 years to save costs. Online services are faster and cheaper. Good modernisation strategy?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Digital-only government services exclude elderly people who aren't digitally literate, disabled users whose accessibility needs aren't met, homeless people without devices, rural communities with poor connectivity, and domestic abuse victims whose devices may be monitored. These are exactly the populations most dependent on government services. The cost savings are real but come at the expense of access for the most vulnerable. The phase-out timeline is too aggressive for the population to adapt.

prompt:
A company implements stack ranking (forced distribution) where managers must rate 10% of their team as 'underperforming' each review cycle, regardless of absolute performance. The bottom 10% get performance improvement plans. The CEO says it 'maintains a high bar'. Good talent management?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad policy. Stack ranking destroys collaboration because colleagues become competitors for the finite pool of good ratings. Managers game the system by hiring people they plan to fire (sacrificial hires). Top performers leave because the system is perceived as unfair. Teams hoard information instead of sharing it. Microsoft famously abandoned this system after recognising it was destroying innovation. The corrosive effects on culture take 2-3 years to fully manifest.

prompt:
A company decides to go fully remote and close all offices to save on real estate costs (£3M/year). Employees are happy about the flexibility. They'll use the savings to increase salaries by 5%. Good strategic move?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Full remote works well for experienced employees with established relationships, but damages onboarding, mentorship, and spontaneous collaboration. New hires take longer to become productive and feel less connected. Junior employees miss the informal learning that happens in offices. Over 2-3 years, culture fragments, silos form, and the company's ability to innovate (which depends on cross-team serendipity) declines. The cost savings are quantifiable; the cultural erosion is not, until attrition patterns reveal it.

prompt:
A non-profit is struggling financially and decides to partner with a gambling company for sponsorship. The gambling company's donation would fund three years of the non-profit's youth education programme. The company only wants logo placement and an annual mention. Should they accept?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. The immediate funding is transformative, but gambling company sponsorship of youth programmes creates a normalisation effect — children associate the brand with positive experiences. Over time, other ethical sponsors may distance themselves, and the non-profit's reputation becomes entangled with the gambling brand. If the gambling company faces scandal or regulatory action, the non-profit is collateral damage. The dependency also gives the sponsor implicit influence over the non-profit's advocacy positions on gambling regulation.

prompt:
A ride-hailing company launches in a new city with fares 50% below cost, subsidised by venture capital, to rapidly gain market share. Users love the cheap rides. Local taxi companies can't compete. Should the city allow this?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Predatory pricing destroys the existing taxi ecosystem. Once competitors are eliminated and market dominance is achieved, prices rise (often above pre-entry levels) — this pattern has played out in every major ride-hailing market. Drivers' earnings decline as the platform takes a larger cut. The city loses regulatory leverage because there's now a single dominant provider. The cheap rides are a short-term transfer from VC funds to riders that ultimately results in a less competitive, more expensive transport market.

prompt:
A country introduces a fast-track visa programme for wealthy immigrants who invest at least $500K in the economy. Applications surge and the programme generates significant capital inflows. Property markets in major cities boom. Good immigration policy?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. Investor visa programmes inflate property prices, pricing out local residents and creating housing affordability crises in major cities. The investments often target real estate rather than productive business, creating asset inflation without job creation. The programme commodifies citizenship, and the capital inflows are often temporary (immigrants may move investments after obtaining permanent residency). Within 5-10 years, housing becomes unaffordable for locals, resentment grows, and the social costs exceed the capital benefits.

prompt:
A scientific journal decides to speed up peer review by using AI to screen papers for methodology, statistical validity, and plagiarism before sending to human reviewers. The AI rejects 30% of submissions outright, freeing reviewer time. Good reform?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Flag concerns. AI screening will systematically reject papers with unconventional methodologies, novel statistical approaches, or interdisciplinary frameworks that don't match standard patterns. Groundbreaking research often looks 'wrong' to pattern-matching systems trained on conventional papers. Over time, the journal's published research converges toward methodological conservatism. The auto-rejection also removes the chance for reviewers to recognise flawed-but-promising ideas and request revisions. Innovation in science comes from the 'weird' papers — automated screening will filter them out.

prompt:
A water utility privatises and the new owner invests heavily in billing systems and customer service but defers maintenance on underground pipes (which aren't visible to customers). Customer satisfaction scores improve and the company reports strong profits. Good management?

What's your recommendation?
correct_answer: Bad management. Deferred infrastructure maintenance is a classic strategy for short-term profit extraction. Underground pipes degrade invisibly until they fail catastrophically — burst mains, water quality contamination, sinkholes. The maintenance debt compounds non-linearly: a £100k repair deferred 5 years becomes a £500k emergency. When failures cluster (as they will, since the entire system was neglected simultaneously), the utility faces a crisis it can't fund because it distributed the maintenance budget as profits. Customers and regulators only notice when the system starts breaking.
